params.text:
Other signs of a stroke

FAST covers the most common signs, but a stroke can show itself in other ways too, and these often come on suddenly. Sudden onset is the key clue: a symptom that appears out of nowhere, without warning, should make you think of a stroke. Being aware of the wider range of signs means you will not miss a stroke that does not fit the FAST pattern.

  • Sudden weakness: or numbness on one side of the body.
  • Sudden confusion: or trouble speaking or understanding.
  • Sudden problems: with vision, balance, or a severe headache.

Watching for the sudden and unexplained

Other warning signs include sudden weakness or numbness down one side of the body, affecting the face, arm or leg; sudden confusion or difficulty speaking or understanding others; sudden trouble with vision in one or both eyes; a sudden loss of balance or coordination, dizziness or difficulty walking; and a sudden severe headache with no known cause, sometimes described as the worst the person has ever had.

You do not need to be certain which sign means what, and you should never talk yourself out of acting because it “might be nothing”. If any of these appear suddenly, treat it as a possible stroke. It is always better to call 999 and be reassured than to wait and let treatable damage become permanent.
